answer:
**For Charles Dunoyer, the "classe industrieuse" (translated here as the "industrious class") designates those individuals or the broader society devoted exclusively to *industry*—defined as the skillful, intelligent application of human faculties to create utilities, values, or wealth without despoiling or coercing others.**[^1] This contrasts sharply with "destructive arts" like theft, conquest, extortion, or privilege, which rely on violence or cunning to seize pre-existing values.[^2] Industry, per its etymology from Latin *industria* (from *intus-struere*, "to construct within"), implies purposeful action on things (or even persons, via education or moral improvement) to enhance utility, encompassing not mere mechanical labor but all productive endeavors executed with dexterity and foresight.[^3]

Dunoyer initially notes the term's broad historical use (even for knavish arts), but argues that as political economy advances, *industry* should be reserved strictly for "inoffensive labors" that enrich without harm: "a man living by industry is... a man occupied with creating utilities of some kind, and, with these utilities, procuring for himself, by loyal and free exchanges, all the other utilities he may need."[^4] In the "purely industrious" social state (§11 of his *Nouveau traité d'économie*, Vol. I), this becomes universal: "each would be occupied with adding to the value of things or of men," via "current labor or... the fruits of a prior industry," yielding "the mass of ideas, of good sentiments, of virtues, of utilities of every kind."[^5]

### Boundaries of the Industrious Class
Dunoyer's concept has clear, principled limits, distinguishing it from narrower economic categories (e.g., excluding manufacturing alone) or exploitative ones:

- **Inclusive scope**: Encompasses *all useful, non-violent labors*, from agriculture and mechanical arts to commerce, sciences, education, arts influencing intellect/imagination/morals, and even government (recast as a "public service" akin to any "industrial enterprise," funded voluntarily like a contract).[^6] No profession is excluded if it produces exchangeable value peacefully: "scientific, literary... industries" are valid alongside "agricultural, manufacturing, commercial."[^7] Vol. II extends this to society's "divers ordres de travaux et de fonctions" (orders of works/functions), all liberated under industrial conditions.[^8]

- **Exclusive boundaries**:
  - **Excludes spoliators/dominators**: Anyone living by "seiz[ing]... the values created by others" (e.g., rulers, monopolists, knights of industry as fraudsters) is outside; they consume the industrious class's output definitively, unlike productive consumers.[^9]
  - **Not mere mechanical toil**: Early uses limited it to "fabrication," but Dunoyer expands it to intellectual/moral production; yet it requires skill/morality, not brute repetition.[^10]
  - **Requires free exchange, no privilege**: Industrial society demands "no man can demand anything... but only by title of a freely agreed-upon payment"; government is "in the same position as the last of the citizens."[^11] Office-seekers or the ambitious (Ch. 10) warp this toward parasitism.
  - **Natural inequalities persist**: Even purely industrious peoples have classes (entrepreneurs, scientists, workers) due to varying faculties, but without violence—misery arises from improvidence/prolificacy, not coercion.[^12]

In sum, the industrious class forms when society reaches its "highest term": universal productive labor/exchange, dissolving masters/slaves/privileged into free producers.[^13] It is not utopian equality but maximal liberty via morality/industry, with government as minimal "public work." The corpus lacks the exact French phrase "classe industrieuse" in English translations (searches returned zero hits), but Dunoyer's exposition in Ch. 11 (and echoes in Comte's works) operationalizes it as the antidote to domination.[^14]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, [Ch. 11, §1](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f1f859).

[^2]: *Ibid.*, [§1, p-4329f5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-4329f5).

[^3]: *Ibid.*, [§1, p-06038f](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-06038f) to [p-cd2259](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-cd2259).

[^4]: *Ibid.*, [§1, p-4329f5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-4329f5).

[^5]: *Ibid.*, [§1, p-f7bdb7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f7bdb7).

[^6]: *Ibid.*, [§2, p-872da7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-872da7).

[^7]: *Ibid.*, [§1, p-5516e9](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-5516e9).

[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [Ch. 13](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/13-iii-des-divers-ordres-de-travaux-et-de-fonctions-q/translation/#p-e38eb8).

[^9]: *VOL I*, Ch. 11 [§1, p-e38eb8 context in Vol II but core in Vol I p-4329f5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-4329f5).

[^10]: *Ibid.*, [§1, p-f45dfb](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-f45dfb).

[^11]: *Ibid.*, [§2, p-872da7](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/11-i-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-872da7).

[^12]: *Ibid.*, Ch. 12 [§1-9](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/12-ii-obstacles-qui-sopposent-encore-a-la-liberte-dan/translation/).

[^13]: *VOL I*, [Introduction, §5](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-ecca74).

[^14]: Searches for "classe industrieuse" yielded no direct English hits in Dunoyer (likely original French phrasing); concept aligns with "industrious classes/peoples" (e.g., [Ch. 0, p-a373e4](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-a373e4); [Ch. 9, p-397aee](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/09-x-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des-/translation/#p-397aee)). Echoed in Comte's *Traité de législation*.
